APPROPRIATENESS.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): This paper addresses a core NLP task (hypernym / hierarchy construction) using methods (embedding-based generative + discriminative models, lexical rules) and evaluations (precision/recall/F1, comparison to established baselines) that are squarely within the scope of top NLP/ML conferences. The comparison to several prior methods and use of a manually labeled test set from Fu et al. (2014) show appropriate audience and evaluation standards, but reporting gaps (unnamed test set, no mention of released code or seeds) and the strong claim of language-independence despite experiments on Chinese only raise reproducibility and scope concerns that prevent a perfect-fit rating. | B(4): This paper addresses hypernym/hierarchy construction from embeddings — a canonical NLP problem that is squarely within top NLP/ML conference scope and will interest the community that studies lexical semantics and taxonomy induction. The methodology (fusion of generative MLP and discriminative RNN, plus lexical-structure rules) and evaluation using precision/recall/F1 against multiple prior baselines are appropriate for the venue, and combining with Wikipedia/CilinE shows practical utility. My main reservations are scope and claim strength: the core contribution appears incremental (an ensemble of existing model types) and the language-independence claim is not convincingly supported given experiments only on Chinese Baidubaike and a single manually labeled test set. Overall this is a good fit but would benefit from broader multilingual evaluation or clearer demonstration of conceptual novelty.

The comparison to several prior methods and use of a manually labeled test set from Fu et al. (2014) show appropriate audience and evaluation standards, but reporting gaps (unnamed test set, no mention of released code or seeds) and the strong claim of language-independence despite experiments on Chinese only raise reproducibility and scope concerns that prevent a perfect-fit rating.

The methodology (fusion of generative MLP and discriminative RNN, plus lexical-structure rules) and evaluation using precision/recall/F1 against multiple prior baselines are appropriate for the venue, and combining with Wikipedia/CilinE shows practical utility. My main reservations are scope and claim strength: the core contribution appears incremental (an ensemble of existing model types) and the language-independence claim is not convincingly supported given experiments only on Chinese Baidubaike and a single manually labeled test set. Overall this is a good fit but would benefit from broader multilingual evaluation or clearer demonstration of conceptual novelty.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(3): The paper provides empirical evidence (F1 74.20%, P 91.60%) and compares against multiple prior methods, and reports hyperparameters, which supports the main claims to a degree. However, important methodological details needed for trust and reproducibility are missing in the summary: the named test set is not specified, code is not provided, and there is no mention of statistical significance, random seeds, or clear train/dev/test splits — all of which raise concerns about possible data leakage and result variance. The fusion-with-manual-hierarchies result (F1 82.01%) plausibly inflates performance via external resources and needs ablation/coverage analysis; overall the experiments are promising but lack some controls and analyses required to fully validate the technical claims. | B(4): The paper's main claims are clearly stated and largely supported by empirical results showing MFusion (and its components) outperform multiple prior baselines on a manually labeled Chinese test set, and by reporting hyperparameters and ablations (MLP, RNN, lexical rule, combination with manual hierarchies). There are minor methodological gaps: only Chinese data is used despite a language-independence claim, the test-set provenance is underspecified, and no statistical significance tests or code release are reported; combining with existing hierarchies also risks inflating gains without isolating the model's contribution. Mathematical content appears light but consistent with the described models. Overall the technical evidence supports the main conclusion, with minor weaknesses that do not overturn it.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper provides empirical evidence (F1 74.20%, P 91.60%) and compares against multiple prior methods, and reports hyperparameters, which supports the main claims to a degree. However, important methodological details needed for trust and reproducibility are missing in the summary: the named test set is not specified, code is not provided, and there is no mention of statistical significance, random seeds, or clear train/dev/test splits — all of which raise concerns about possible data leakage and result variance. The fusion-with-manual-hierarchies result (F1 82.01%) plausibly inflates performance via external resources and needs ablation/coverage analysis; overall the experiments are promising but lack some controls and analyses required to fully validate the technical claims.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper's main claims are clearly stated and largely supported by empirical results showing MFusion (and its components) outperform multiple prior baselines on a manually labeled Chinese test set, and by reporting hyperparameters and ablations (MLP, RNN, lexical rule, combination with manual hierarchies). There are minor methodological gaps: only Chinese data is used despite a language-independence claim, the test-set provenance is underspecified, and no statistical significance tests or code release are reported; combining with existing hierarchies also risks inflating gains without isolating the model's contribution. Mathematical content appears light but consistent with the described models. Overall the technical evidence supports the main conclusion, with minor weaknesses that do not overturn it.

IMPACT.justification: [Consensus avg=3] A(3): The paper tackles an important practical problem (automatic hypernym/hierarchy construction) and presents a pragmatic fusion of generative and discriminative models that yields notably better empirical performance on a manually labeled Chinese test set (F1 74.20%, P 91.60%) and further gains when merged with manual hierarchies (F1 82.01%). From my methodological perspective the approach is likely to be useful to researchers and practitioners working with embedding-based taxonomy induction because it shows clear gains over a wide range of baselines and reports hyperparameters. However, impact is moderated by limited reproducibility details (code not mentioned, test set name unspecified) and the dependency on language-specific resources/coverage choices, so I judge the contribution as moderately influential rather than field-changing. | B(3): The paper tackles an important problem—automatic construction of semantic hierarchies—and reports strong empirical gains (F1=74.20%, P=91.60%; up to 82.01% when fused with Wikipedia and CilinE). Its main contribution—a fusion of generative (MLP) and discriminative (RNN) components plus a simple lexical rule—offers a practical architecture that practitioners can build on, and suggests clear follow-ups (multilingual validation, integration with existing taxonomies). However, the claim of language-independence is not empirically supported beyond Chinese embeddings and the approach largely refines rather than rethinks the space, so the impact is useful and likely to be adopted by researchers in hypernym discovery and taxonomy induction but not transformative for the broader field.

From my methodological perspective the approach is likely to be useful to researchers and practitioners working with embedding-based taxonomy induction because it shows clear gains over a wide range of baselines and reports hyperparameters. However, impact is moderated by limited reproducibility details (code not mentioned, test set name unspecified) and the dependency on language-specific resources/coverage choices, so I judge the contribution as moderately influential rather than field-changing.

However, the claim of language-independence is not empirically supported beyond Chinese embeddings and the approach largely refines rather than rethinks the space, so the impact is useful and likely to be adopted by researchers in hypernym discovery and taxonomy induction but not transformative for the broader field.

ORIGINALITY.justification: [Consensus avg=3] A(3): The paper proposes a fusion learning architecture (MFusion) that combines a generative MLP (hypernym vector projection), a discriminative Elman RNN classifier, and a simple lexical-structure rule, then merges outputs with manual hierarchies. This design is a clear, practical differentiation from prior single-paradigm methods (patterns, distributional measures, web mining, or embeddings) but largely composes existing components rather than introducing fundamentally new models or theoretical perspectives. From my methodology-focused view, the contribution is a non-trivial engineering and empirical integration that yields improved performance, but it reads as an incremental advance rather than a highly original conceptual breakthrough. | B(3): The paper proposes MFusion, which combines a generative MLP projection with a discriminative Elman RNN and a simple lexical rule; this architectural fusion is a reasonably new combination but builds directly on well-established embedding projection and discriminative classification approaches for hypernym discovery. The problem (semantic hierarchy construction) is well-studied and the authors compare against relevant baselines (pattern-, distributional-, web-mining-, and embedding-based methods), and they clearly differentiate their fusion setup and ablations (MLPgen, RNNdis, MMLP+RNN). Overall the technical contribution appears to be a moderate, non-trivial engineering/architectural extension rather than a fundamentally new formulation, so the novelty is incremental but of interest to practitioners.

The problem (semantic hierarchy construction) is well-studied and the authors compare against relevant baselines (pattern-, distributional-, web-mining-, and embedding-based methods), and they clearly differentiate their fusion setup and ablations (MLPgen, RNNdis, MMLP+RNN). Overall the technical contribution appears to be a moderate, non-trivial engineering/architectural extension rather than a fundamentally new formulation, so the novelty is incremental but of interest to practitioners.

SUBSTANCE.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(3): The paper presents reasonable empirical comparisons against a wide set of prior methods and includes component-level results (MLP, RNN, MLP+RNN and a lexical-rule), so some ablation is present and the main claims are partially supported. However, key reproducibility details are missing or incomplete in the summary: the manually labeled test set is not named, there is no mention of train/dev/test splits, random seeds, code release, or statistical significance tests, and runtime/hardware and data-preprocessing specifics are not reported. Related work coverage appears thorough and the authors note at least one limitation of their model, but the experimental suite lacks sensitivity analyses (e.g., effect of embedding size, corpus variations) and clearer provenance for combined-hierarchy results. Given these strengths and gaps, the experimental evaluation is adequate but misses important details needed to fully trust and reproduce the claims. | B(4): The paper provides solid experimental evidence (multiple baselines, component-level comparisons MMLPgen / MRNNdis / MMLP+RNN, and combination with manual hierarchies) and reports hyperparameters, which supports most claims. It also includes some failure analysis (projection failures) and situates itself well in related work. Missing are broader sensitivity analyses (e.g., cross-lingual or corpus-variation tests to substantiate the language-independence claim) and full reproducibility resources (code and an explicit public name for the manually labeled test set are not provided). These are important but do not invalidate the main results, so the evaluation is strong overall with minor gaps.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper presents reasonable empirical comparisons against a wide set of prior methods and includes component-level results (MLP, RNN, MLP+RNN and a lexical-rule), so some ablation is present and the main claims are partially supported. Related work coverage appears thorough and the authors note at least one limitation of their model, but the experimental suite lacks sensitivity analyses (e.g., effect of embedding size, corpus variations) and clearer provenance for combined-hierarchy results. Given these strengths and gaps, the experimental evaluation is adequate but misses important details needed to fully trust and reproduce the claims.

These are important but do not invalidate the main results, so the evaluation is strong overall with minor gaps.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper evaluates against a broad set of prior methods (pattern-based MPattern, web-mining MWeb, distributional/invCL, embedding-based MEmb, and lexical-rule baselines) and uses the same manually labeled test set as Fu et al. (2014) with standard Precision/Recall/F1 metrics, which supports fair head-to-head comparison. The authors ablate components (MLP generative, RNN discriminative, fused model) and report combination gains with manual hierarchies, showing component-level differences. However, the summary does not mention statistical significance testing, release of code/outputs, or whether all training/evaluation conditions (e.g., negative sampling, thresholds, seeds) were matched exactly, so while comparisons are strong and related work coverage is thorough, a few reproducibility details and significance analyses appear missing. | B(4): The paper compares MFusion against a broad set of appropriate baselines (pattern-based, distributional, web-mining, embedding-based) and includes ablations (MLP generative, RNN discriminative, lexical-rule, and their combinations). Comparisons appear fair — they use the same manually labeled test set (Fu et al., 2014) and standard metrics (P/R/F1) — and the authors report fusion gains and joint results with manual hierarchies. While related work coverage is described as thorough and most relevant competing approaches are discussed, the paper could be clearer about cases where prior methods match or surpass MFusion and about any dataset- or resource-specific advantages, so it stops short of a fully comprehensive comparative analysis.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper evaluates against a broad set of prior methods (pattern-based MPattern, web-mining MWeb, distributional/invCL, embedding-based MEmb, and lexical-rule baselines) and uses the same manually labeled test set as Fu et al. (2014) with standard Precision/Recall/F1 metrics, which supports fair head-to-head comparison. The authors ablate components (MLP generative, RNN discriminative, fused model) and report combination gains with manual hierarchies, showing component-level differences. However, the summary does not mention statistical significance testing, release of code/outputs, or whether all training/evaluation conditions (e.g., negative sampling, thresholds, seeds) were matched exactly, so while comparisons are strong and related work coverage is thorough, a few reproducibility details and significance analyses appear missing.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper compares MFusion against a broad set of appropriate baselines (pattern-based, distributional, web-mining, embedding-based) and includes ablations (MLP generative, RNN discriminative, lexical-rule, and their combinations). Comparisons appear fair — they use the same manually labeled test set (Fu et al., 2014) and standard metrics (P/R/F1) — and the authors report fusion gains and joint results with manual hierarchies. While related work coverage is described as thorough and most relevant competing approaches are discussed, the paper could be clearer about cases where prior methods match or surpass MFusion and about any dataset- or resource-specific advantages, so it stops short of a fully comprehensive comparative analysis.

CLARITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): Overall the paper is well organized and the main contributions are stated clearly early (MFusion architecture, combination with manual hierarchies, language-independence claim). Writing quality is reported as good and related work and hyperparameters are well documented, which supports reproducibility. Minor presentation gaps remain: the manually labeled test set is referenced but not named, and the summary does not indicate whether figures/tables or notation are consistently defined, so some readers may need more explicit dataset/code pointers and clearer definitions of symbols. These are small issues that keep it from a perfect score but do not impede understanding of the core ideas. | B(4): The paper is overall well organized (intro → method → experiments → conclusion) and states its main contributions early, with clear descriptions of the MFusion architecture and experimental setup on Baidubaike and manual test data. Notation appears consistent and hyperparameters are reported; related work coverage is thorough and the writing quality is described as good. A few presentation gaps remain (e.g., the manual test set name is not given, and code availability is not mentioned), so while clarity is high, minor improvements to dataset descriptions and reproducibility details would make the paper exceptional.

reviewer_a.justification: Overall the paper is well organized and the main contributions are stated clearly early (MFusion architecture, combination with manual hierarchies, language-independence claim). Writing quality is reported as good and related work and hyperparameters are well documented, which supports reproducibility. Minor presentation gaps remain: the manually labeled test set is referenced but not named, and the summary does not indicate whether figures/tables or notation are consistently defined, so some readers may need more explicit dataset/code pointers and clearer definitions of symbols. These are small issues that keep it from a perfect score but do not impede understanding of the core ideas.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper is overall well organized (intro → method → experiments → conclusion) and states its main contributions early, with clear descriptions of the MFusion architecture and experimental setup on Baidubaike and manual test data. Notation appears consistent and hyperparameters are reported; related work coverage is thorough and the writing quality is described as good. A few presentation gaps remain (e.g., the manual test set name is not given, and code availability is not mentioned), so while clarity is high, minor improvements to dataset descriptions and reproducibility details would make the paper exceptional.

main_claims: Propose a fusion learning architecture (MFusion) that combines a generative MLP and a discriminative RNN plus a simple lexical-structure rule to construct semantic hierarchies from word embeddings.

MFusion achieves strong empirical performance on a manually labeled test set (F1 = 74.20%, Precision = 91.60%), outperforming several prior methods based on patterns, distributional measures, web mining, and embeddings.
Combining MFusion with manually-built hierarchies (Wikipedia and CilinE) further improves F1 to 82.01%.
The fusion learning architecture is claimed to be language-independent.
_summary.methodology: Train word embeddings (Skip-gram) on a large Chinese encyclopedia corpus (Baidubaike), then apply a generative model (MLP) to generate hypernym vectors and a discriminative model (Elman RNN) to classify hyponym–candidate-hypernym sequences; fuse their outputs and optionally apply a simple lexical-structure rule and merge with manual hierarchies.
datasets: Manually labeled test dataset (same test set as Fu et al. (2014); name not given)
limitations: The method can fail to learn some hypernym–hyponym relations when the pair representations are very close in embedding space and the model cannot find a suitable projection.
Manually-built hierarchies have limited coverage, so combination is needed to further improve recall/coverage.

reviewer_a.name: Methodology-focused ML Engineer
reviewer_a.background: Senior ML engineer with 8+ years building and evaluating NLP systems for research and production; has authored reproducible experimental suites and benchmarks for hierarchy induction and relation extraction.
traits: checks experimental design details (train/dev/test splits, data leakage, hyperparameter search) and values reproducibility artifacts (code, seeds, exact preprocessing)
requires strong baselines, ablation studies, and statistical significance tests to support claims of improvement
looks for clarity about model capacity and training (architectures, optimizer, learning curves, runtime and hardware), and notices overfitting or leakages between components
values careful evaluation procedures: clear definitions of metrics, thresholding, negative sampling, and error analysis broken down by frequency/ambiguity
reviewer_a.reviewing_style: Practical, detail-oriented reviewer who focuses on whether the experiments and reporting are sufficient for trusting the empirical claims and for reproducing the work.

reviewer_b.name: Domain-impact Senior Researcher
reviewer_b.background: Associate professor in computational linguistics with a long track record on lexical semantics and lexical resources; has developed and evaluated semantic hierarchies, knowledge bases, and multilingual lexical ontologies.
traits: assesses novelty and positioning relative to prior work in hypernym discovery, taxonomy induction, and embedding-based methods and values clear conceptual contributions
evaluates broader applicability and impact (language-independence claims, integration with existing resources, and utility for downstream tasks)
notices whether evaluation datasets and metrics reflect real-world use cases and values qualitative analyses (case studies, error categories) that reveal method strengths/weaknesses
looks for transparency about limitations and appropriate claims about scalability, multilinguality, and resource-dependence
reviewer_b.reviewing_style: High-level, contextual reviewer who judges the paper on novelty, fit in the literature, and potential community impact while expecting evidence to support generalization claims.
